data.title: Sodexo: Guidance Cuts Erode Investment Case (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In our last update, we reiterated a buy rating for Sodexo S.A. (SDXOF) (SDXAY). This was due to a decline in the share price and lower guidance for 2026. Additionally, we were more pessimistic in our 2026 margin forecast. That said, we liked the company’s focus on the US, ongoing IT investments, and sector consolidation. Our buy rating was also supported by attractive valuations, along with support for dividend payments and potential buybacks. Thanks to strong free cash flow, we underwrote a positive investment case. With several of our initial assumptions no longer valid and following a 23% decline in the share price since we initiated coverage (Fig. 1), we decided to move our rating to Neutral. While this is not our preferred outcome, we believe a more cautious stance is warranted at this stage.

Fig. 1

H1 Results and Our Neutral Stance

Sodexo reported top-line sales of €12.1 billion, with organic growth of around 2%, but a change vs. H1 2025 of -3.7% (Fig. 2). Sales were -0.5% below Wall Street consensus, with underlying operating profit of €442 million, again -19% below consensus at €547 million. Sales growth was largely driven by pricing rather than volume, but was impacted by negative FX effects, particularly from the US dollar. On the GEO basis, results across regions were mixed. Europe was supportive thanks to new contracts, while North America, the group’s largest market, is under pressure due to prior contract losses and softer demand in key segments. This led to a lower operating profit margin that reached 3.7%, below expectations.

Fig. 2

Why are we now neutral?

At a high level, nearly all indicators show a decline versus the prior-year period.

First of all, almost every ratio is lower than the same period last year. However, on a 12-month view, top-line sales are now expected to be in the range of 0.5-1.0% (from a previous outlook of 1.5-2.5%). This reflects a weaker commercial momentum. More importantly, in our last update, we reported a larger cut in the EBIT margin, with a 30-basis-point contraction. We were not confident in the wording regarding a “slight” contraction. This left an unclear starting point, but we hope for procurement efficiencies and lower cost inflation. Considering that, we anticipated a core operating profit margin of 4.4%. With the current guidance, operating profit margins are expected to be between 3.2% and 3.4% (Fig. 3), while other operating expenses are expected to be -€300 million (down from a previous estimate of -€200 million). This is the second cut in guidance in a short timeframe, but the magnitude of the cut will likely lead to very material reductions in consensus EPS forecasts. These margins are expected to decline as the company continues to invest in operational improvements and IT infrastructure. This is not a new piece of evidence, but given a less favorable business mix, earnings are expected to decline.

Fig. 3

Additionally, the company's leverage is expected to be above the group's 1.0-2.0x Net Debt/EBITDA target. With lower EBITDA and a reversal of working capital, the company's net debt increased to 2.7x vs. 2.3x last year (Fig. 4). Therefore, in our view, this removes the previously anticipated buyback potential, despite solid FCF generation. This buyback (in a depressed share-price environment) was the catalyst we were hoping for. With a higher debt, even opportunistic M&A are likely less favorable. Compared to our previous forecast, there are also two key negative takeaways: a higher net financial cost expectation of €140 million and an effective tax rate that moved from 19.5% to 25.9%.

Fig. 4

Earnings Change and Valuation

In our last assessment, we anticipated sales of around €24.5 billion and an EBIT margin of 4.4%. This led to an EBIT of €1.08 billion. At this stage, Sodexo guides sales at 0.5-1.0% and an operating profit margin of 3.2-3.4%. Assuming this guidance, Sodexo's turnover and EBIT should reach €24.1 billion and €795 million, respectively. Considering the current interest expense (€140 million) and the higher effective tax rate, we estimate the net profit of €484.7 million. This results in an EPS of €3.3 with the current outstanding shares. Currently, the company trades at a P/E of 12x and, given the current net debt (€3.5 billion), at a 6.8x EV/EBITDA. While the company's screening is inexpensive on an EV/EBITDA multiple, the P/E ratio looks aligned with the company's historical average. That said, we had a margin of safety with the EV/EBITDA target multiple set at 8x previously, but given the company's new outlook, this has been significantly reduced. Again, this second consecutive guidance cut will weigh on Wall Street sentiment. Blending our valuation with a P/E of 10x (target price of €33 per share), and an EV/EBITDA of 8x (€51.2 per share), we set the Sodexo target price at €42.1 ($9.9 in ADR). Therefore, we moved our rating to neutral (the company's price is at €39.9 per share at the time of writing).

Risks

Sodexo's downside risks include negative FX (as seen in the H1 2026 results) and a deterioration in employment levels in Europe and the US. The new CEO increased the other operating expenses by €100 million. These are potential additional restructuring costs. As noted in our coverage initiation, the company engages subcontractors. While this allows Sodexo to serve as a single point of contact, service quality may suffer. Sodexo's main risks also include wage inflation and further increases in raw material prices, especially for food costs, which represent c50% of revenue.

Conclusion

Well, lower guidance, no upside from M&A, and no more buyback (given a higher leverage) limit our positive stance on the company. Compared to our last analysis, there is limited room for valuation upside. So, we prefer to remain neutral at this stage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lucid: Robotaxi Deal And BOM Cost Reductions To Drive Upside
data.description:
With the stock market vaulting up to new highs, my personal view is that right now is hardly a time for complacency in the stock market. I'm still emphasizing active trading and portfolio selection, with a focus on next-gen growth stories that are being ignored in the stock market today. The AI hardware/semiconductor trade, in my view, has gotten crowded and expensive; autonomous driving and EVs, meanwhile, are not.

Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), in particular, is looking quite appealing after its share price dropped ~30% since the start of the year (compared to a ~10% drop for Tesla and a ~15% decline in Rivian). Arguably, Lucid is the U.S. EV maker that has made the biggest strides in terms of closing deals and securing the path toward its next-gen mid-size vehicle, which is still slated to start production in the back half of this year.

I last wrote a buy article on Lucid in December, when the stock was trading at ~$11 per share. Of course, my buy call was ill-timed. But though I acknowledge that Lucid is a riskier endeavor that requires us to bet on its future product roadmap, a lot has moved in the positive direction for Lucid in the past few months, including signing major new partnerships. Trading at under a $3 billion market cap (versus $20 billion for Rivian and $1.2 trillion for Tesla), I believe the company's growth prospects are severely underappreciated, and I'm reiterating my buy call on this name.

Uber and the robotaxi market opportunity

To me, the largest recent announcement that Lucid made was an expanded partnership with Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER). Uber is starting to make major strides in robotaxis. It has a more measured approach to market entry (it has an active presence in Atlanta and Austin), behind the rollout of rivals like Waymo. At the same time, Uber just released new data that showcases that of all the autonomous/robotaxi companies, AV utilization is highest on Uber's platform. This makes perfect sense: Uber already has the largest rideshare network in the U.S., whereas Waymo requires a fresh new app download and is a distant competitor in terms of ride volume.

As shown below, Uber notes that AVs in service on the Uber network have 30% higher utilization/trips per day, while the Uber app offers an ETA that is 25% faster than its peers.

With this early lead captured, Uber has restated its intention of becoming the largest autonomous vehicle network in the U.S. by 2029. Of course, Uber isn't the AV technology developer, and it's striking partnerships to source AV supply. One of the biggest recent catalysts for Lucid is a recent (April 14) announcement from Uber that it would expand its commitment to Lucid. Uber is now investing $500 million into the company (up from $300 million previously) and has now committed to a mid-term order size of 35,000 robotaxi vehicles from Lucid, versus 20,000 vehicles in the original deal.

35k units may be small peanuts for an EV maker like Tesla, but it's a major deal for Lucid. For scale comparison, note that Lucid is only guiding to 25-27k production unit volumes for FY26. Lucid had always clearly occupied the high-end, luxury EV brand tier, but it was missing a top-tier commercial partner. In my view, the Uber deal allows investors to start seeing Lucid more in a light similar to Rivian, which has a similar commercial deal with Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) to supply 100k electric delivery vans by 2030.

Lucid has pinned the global robotaxi market at a very wide scope. Excluding China (and one of its main AV companies, Pony AI Inc. (PONY)), the company is only expecting ~20k robotaxi deliveries by 2027, at $5 billion in annual sales (~$250k ASP per unit). By 2035, it's expected to have $300 billion in annual sales and 590k units.

The company has released early details on its pure robotaxi product, which it's calling “Lunar.” The car is expected to hit 5.5-6.0 miles per kilowatt hour (versus 4.8 miles for a standard Tesla Model 3 today), reduce operating costs for rideshare platforms by 40%, and charge up to 200 miles in 15 minutes.

In other news, Lucid just appointed a new CEO: Silvio Napoli, the Swiss chief executive of elevator maker Schindler Group, which generated CHF 11 billion in revenue (~$14 billion) and CHF 1.1 billion in net profit in 2025 (~$1.4 billion), with the company's current CEO, Marc Winterhoff, moving down to a COO role. The fact that Lucid landed a major industrial CEO to lead Lucid's commercialization stage strengthens its path to success.

Cost reductions and extended liquidity runway

Of course, we also need to state the obvious: Lucid's robotaxi sales and even its mid-sized vehicle program (the first of three mid-size vehicles, the Lucid Cosmos, is expected to hit the road in 2027 after ramping up production this year) are still very future-looking. To assess the feasibility of these goals, we need to look into Lucid's ability to drive cost reductions and to keep its balance sheet padded with substantial liquidity until it can generate positive cash flow.

For FY26, Lucid is still expecting negative gross margins (most recently in Q4, the company delivered an -81% gross margin) as it's still a very small-scale manufacturer. But recently, the company also laid out a comprehensive list of all the drivers that can help bridge it to a positive profit. Robotaxi sales, which we discussed in the previous section, alongside software revenue, are expected to drive higher margins for minimal capex.

But the majority of the improvements in profitability will come from Lucid's ability to scale its bill of materials (BOM). Over a three-year timeframe, the company has identified an opportunity to slice down 30-40% of the BOM for Lucid Gravity, the company's current $79,900 luxury vehicle. Compared to the still-unreleased SUV on the company's mid-size platform (which will include three vehicles, including the announced ~$50k Lucid Cosmos), it believes it can reduce its BOM by 60-70%.

The other key consideration for Lucid is sufficient liquidity, and we think the company passes this test quite well. As of the end of FY25, the company had $4.6 billion in total liquidity, which consisted of $2.1 billion in cash and the remainder in undrawn debt facilities. This is enough liquidity for Lucid to stretch out to 1H'27, including the planned $1.2-$1.4 billion in capex for FY26.

We note that this liquidity balance does not yet include the recently announced $500 million from Uber, as well as an additional $550 million from the Ayar Third Investment Company, an affiliate of the Saudi sovereign wealth fund. We presume that this additional ~$1.05 billion of capital bridges Lucid until at least the end of FY27 as long as its current burn projections hold, through the initial planned launch of Lucid Cosmos.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, plenty of risk rides on a small EV maker that is only the third-largest in the U.S. Execution will be of tantamount importance, and there's no guarantee that Lucid will hit its targeted BOM cost reductions or its sales goals for the mid-size product. And while we note the Uber order for 35k Lucid robotaxis is certainly a signal of faith in Lucid's platform, we note Uber has a diversified list of partners it's enlisting in its AV rollout, including classic carmakers like Mercedes-Benz.

If Lucid doesn't perform to expectations in road testing, Uber could deepen its ties to a competitor.

That said, we think Lucid is appealing after a 25%+ slide this year, and its small <$3 billion market cap reflects an opportunistic buying window. Stay long here.

data.title: Constellation Energy: Creating More Visibility For Investors
data.description:
Constellation Energy (CEG) has been trading flat since I last covered shares in June of last year, thereby underperforming the wider market and many other AI players and derivatives of the boom a great deal.

I called it an interesting and volatile utility play, driven by AI trends as well as many developments on the corporate front, including a $29 billion deal for Calpine and PPAs being signed with high-profile technology names.

After hitting a high around the $400 mark in the fall and a low around the $250 mark in February, shares are back to the $300 mark. Such moves, a recent divestment, and a much anticipated outlook warrant a small update here.

Other interesting power and AI ideas can be found in the investment group Value In Corporate Events.

Recent Trends

Towards the end of the first quarter, Constellation reached an agreement with LS Power Equity Advisors LLC to sell part of its generation assets to LS Power. This deal needed to be done to satisfy regulators in connection with the purchase of Calpine.

The transaction involves the sale of 4.4 gigawatts of mostly natural gas-fired generation capacity in Delaware and Pennsylvania, with the deal valued at $5.0 billion, equal to $1,142/kW. This follows the resolution with the DOJ achieved in December, with this marking the majority of the resolution needed to be delivered upon, but not all.

The sale followed the release of the 2025 results, as announced in February. While reported sales rose by more than 8% to $25.5 billion, it was higher fuel bills that made operating profits of $4.4 billion decline to $3.1 billion, equal to $7.40 per share. If, however, fair value adjustments are stripped out, adjusted earnings came in at $2.9 billion, equal to $9.39 per share, with these earnings up seventy-two cents on the year before.

Ahead of the Calpine deal closing, which took place in the first week of January, the company ended the year with some $2 billion in net cash. However, the definition of net cash is quite difficult to define for a utility business.

Back to My Summer's Take

When I looked at shares of Constellation early last summer, shares had just enjoyed a 60% rally in the time frame of just a few months. The market at large was showing a big recovery at the time as shares benefited from deals with Microsoft and Meta, as well as a $29 billion deal for Calpine, seen as highly accretive to 2026 earnings.

This would add meaningfully to over 32,000 megawatts of generating capacity at the time, the vast majority of which was nuclear, used to power some 20 million consumer and business accounts. Commanding an $84 billion enterprise valuation at $250 in January of 2025, when the company announced a $29 billion deal for Calpine, the deal would involve the issuance of about 50 million shares (based on levels at the time), with some $17 billion in debt being assumed.

Taking place at cheaper valuation multiples, the deal would expand natural gas generation capacity a great deal, seen adding over $2 per share to earnings in 2026, creating a roadmap for shares to advance to $11 per share.

Based on that outlook, quite some leverage was to be taken on, and some time out until closing, and I was naturally a bit cautious, also as shares had seen strong momentum. Meanwhile, the company continues to see solid advancements in 2025 results, with some deleveraging achieved ahead of the Calpine deal closing, as not all benefits from the deal are seen here. After all, a recent regulatory-induced divestment of $5 billion is rather large in relation to a $29 billion deal.

Looking Forward

On the final day of the quarter, Constellation provided its formal 2026 outlook, and the earnings guidance was quite comforting, I must say. Earnings were seen between $11 and $12 per share, even though those consensus estimates were a bit higher than the midpoint of the guidance. Capital spending is seen at $3.9 billion, this being quite a modest number for a firm of this size of Constellation, compared to about a billion in regulatory depreciation charges, or $2.6 billion if nuclear and energy contract amortization charges are included.

Higher than expected earnings come at 30%-40% of total earnings. These are so-called "enhanced" earnings, those not subject to the same base rates as the majority of the production. With forward pricing curves and margins above long-term averages, such enhanced earnings are really enhanced these days, with more earnings growth seen in the coming years.

This is quite encouraging, as a $300 stock now trades around 25-27 times forward earnings, although Constellation will be leveraged a bit following the Calpine deal closing, offset by the recent divestment and net cash position held ahead of closing.

With dividends of just over 0.5% providing little yield, such capital spending, M&A, and regular capex can rather easily be paid for, it must be said. Based on current conditions, the company guided for 2029 base earnings to advance to $11.40-$11.90 per share, making up 65%-70% of earnings that year. At the midpoint of the guidance that suggests earnings north of $17 per share, which implies that shares trade around 18 times forward earnings, that is three years ahead in time.

And Now?

In between a resilient performance of the business and larger divestments in relation to the Calpine deal, there are some positives and negatives, but frankly the overall performance feels relatively strong. With shares lagging and net debt seen coming down a bit, notably after recent asset sales, this makes me a bit more upbeat.

Moreover, while the current earnings multiples in the mid- to higher-20s are a bit elevated, the 2029 roadmap provides quite some visibility into earnings here, and beyond visibility, these earnings are seen rising rapidly in the coming years.

This makes me upbeat with visibility seen and continued strength, as expectations have come in a little here. That does not mean that I am buying shares first-hand over fist here, but Constellation seems more investable here on dips, making me willing to get involved around the $250 mark.

data.title: Talos Energy: Unlocking Hidden Value Beneath The Gulf
data.description:
Overview

Talos Energy (TALO) is an independent exploration and production company focused on offshore oil and natural gas assets in the gulf of United States and offshore Mexico. It specializes in acquiring, developing and optimizing high-quality upstream assets with the objective of maximizing long-term shareholder value via disciplined capital allocation and operational excellence. Both of these are important as they are important in allowing Talos Energy to recover maximum crude oil and natural gas from the existing assets.

Talos Energy operates in United States Gulf of America and Offshore Mexico and is one of the largest players and acreage holders in the gulf basin. Key producing assets of Talos include Katmai Fields, Pompano & Cardona Fields, Ram Powell & Venice/Lime Rock and Galapagos & Brutus Fields. Ownership of these assets provide stability in production, infrastructure access and development opportunities. In Mexico, Talos has maintained a strategic presence through the involvement in the Zama discovery which is one of the most significant offshore finds in the recent decade. This presents a significant opportunity as a value catalyst.

Some of the key metrics worth noting are given below for Talos Energy that provides a glimpse of the scale of Talos Energy.

Talos Energy differentiates itself through technical expertise, operational efficiency and disciplined financial management and the company’s target is to deliver both short-term and long-term growth.

Some of the core competencies of Talos include the technical expertise pertaining to offshore operations, cost leadership, high-quality asset base, strong cash flow potential and balanced growth strategy.

So, Talos Energy presents an investment opportunity for investors who are looking to park money in the energy and oil & gas space. While Talos is actively looking at opportunities of growth through exploration and acquiring of existing assets, it is also looking at scale and operation efficiency which is aimed at reducing costs.

Tailwind

Structural Benefits of Offshore Production and Investor Interest

Talos Energy is involved in offshore production of crude oil and natural gas. Offshore drilling requires higher technical expertise and involves higher set-up costs due to the more complex nature and deepwater drilling rather than fracking and horizontal drilling. It is therefore expected that the time to start for a new project is longer than shale operations. It does not stop here. Offshore drilling also involves heavier regulation due to multiple reasons like environmental safety, worker safety risks, and scale of disaster in case of an accident. All of these factors act as high barriers to entry for a new player which protects the incumbent player in the industry.

However, offshore production has inherent advantages over shale operations which involves drilling underground rock beds. One of the major ones is that of longer production life and therefore, shorter decline. This assures a player like Talos of stable revenues as it has several fields and assets under ownership. Moreover, another benefit of offshore projects is the higher margin that it delivers as a player reaches the capability and scale in this space. Therefore, per unit profit is higher for a player like Talos which has reached the minimum level of scale. Moreover, Talos Energy is proactively undertaking exploration for newer assets.

As previously mentioned, Talos Energy holds 194 MMBOE while their annual production is 92.6 MBOE/d which implies that continuing at this level of production, Talos Energy has almost 5-6 years of production potential. With active measures to discover newer reserves, this implies that there is sufficient assurance of availability of crude oil and there will be no supply constraints from Talos.

Apart from this, discovery of newer reserves also contributes towards valuation re-rating as discovery of reserves leads to more optimistic projection of cash flows which leads to higher intrinsic value in the valuation exercise which further adds to the bullish expectation from Talos. With discovery of newer reserves, the addition to the asset base leads to higher cash flows for a DCF model and also higher operating profits due to higher revenue figures. Moreover, one of the defining characteristics of Talos is its infrastructure-led development which leads to lower capex, faster time-to-production and higher IRR.

Proactive Cost Leadership Strategy

One of the biggest tailwinds in favour of Talos is its proactive stance on cost leadership through the multiple initiatives that it has undertaken. Talos has positioned itself among top five independent GoM producers, behind larger diversified firms such as Occidental and Hess. Talos has consistently demonstrated superior cost discipline and efficiency with its operating costs being 30% lower than its peers. The reasons for the cost advantage are multi-fold. They include strategic ownership of assets and access to offshore infrastructure, achieving economies of scale through acquisitions, continuous process optimization and technological integration.

The lean cost structure and continuous efficiency improvement both fit in well with the kind of industry that Talos is currently operating in. The oil & gas exploration & production is one which can be classified as an elephant according to the literature work by Prof Saral Mukherjee of IIM Ahmedabad in his book on Elephants and Cheetahs. According to the literature, elephants are types of businesses which depend heavily on scale and volume and their northstar is to achieve cost optimization with maximum utilization. An additional defining characteristic is the high upfront investment required in the business. Oil & gas industry is the classic example of one such industry where volume and scale are key and offshore production is especially known for high upfront investment.

In such an industry, Talos is leading by example in cost optimization and operational efficiency where these are the very evaluation metrics of an elephant business. This in itself provides an indication about how well it is performing in the industry in comparison to its peers. Therefore, this may be an indication of why Talos may be a superior investment choice for an investor looking to park his/her money in the O&G exploration & production space.

Energy Security Trend & Crude Oil Price

One of the external trends in favour of Talos Energy and other E&P companies is the recent sharp rise in crude oil prices. The figure below gives the trend in crude oil futures price over 10 years.

The graph shows that the prices have risen sharply recently and that acts as a strong short-term tailwind for the earnings of Talos Energy. However, given the current geopolitical scenario, oil prices may continue to remain high and therefore, the earnings may continue to remain high due to the high prices. Moreover, given the price-inelastic nature of the commodity, higher prices are likely to ensure more revenue as well.

Concerns over potential disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz – through which approximately 20% of global crude oil supply passes – have heightened supply risks and contributed to upward pressure on crude oil prices. In addition, the airstrikes by Iran have disrupted the production facilities at several oil facilities. All these factors have together disrupted oil supply and at its peak it involved 11 million barrels/day. This is reflected in the sudden spike in the oil futures price as well.

While supply has contracted, demand continues to remain resilient due to the high demand from emerging economies and continued dependence on petrochemicals. However, given the volatile geopolitical situation, countries are moving towards energy security as well and here the gulf areas of America offer an alternative and more stable energy supply vis-a-vis the middle east and Russia.

Financials & Valuation

The figure below gives the trend of the financials in terms of revenue and profit margins. Both the sales and profit margins experienced a severe dip in FY20 driven by the contraction in demand due to covid. But post that, both recovered quickly. The gross profit margin and operating margin has remained relatively stable, underlining the company’s commitment towards operational efficiency. Moreover, sales has grown rapidly post covid, indicating the continued exploration initiatives as well as the global demand for crude oil.

The graph above further highlights the sensitivity of the revenue and profits of Talos Energy to crude oil prices. This is an additional reason that the latest oil price spike is likely to have a positive impact.

In order to gain an understanding of what could be the true per share value of Talos Energy, I decided to go ahead with a valuation of the company based on its business. Given the nature of the business, I decided to go ahead with the net asset value method since the cash flows of O&G exploration & production business depends on the ownership of the reserves. Therefore the method I followed was to perform a ten year present value calculation method to understand the value accruing to equity holders. The calculation is highlighted below. All the values added here have been gathered based on the annual report, earnings report and relevant disclosures from Talos Energy itself.

The NAV per share provides a mere upside of 2.20%. However, with the recent reserve discovery and possible share repurchases, this provides sufficient justification for investors to park their money in Talos Energy, backed by the tailwinds that support the business prospects as well.

Headwinds

High Capital Intensity, Long Project Timelines and Reserve Replacement Risk

As previously explained, the O&G exploration & production industry is one which is highly capital intensive and has long project timelines. This is especially the case for offshore drilling which involves greater complexity. There are three defining characteristics like significant upfront capital expenditure, translating to high fixed costs. Moreover, there are extended project timelines, implying that it takes several years from discovery to first production. Therefore, given the high upfront costs, extended project timelines, the profitability of Talos is highly sensitive to oil price fluctuations which is a major external risk for O&G companies in the current situation.

Moreover given the high funding requirement, offshore drilling players are sensitive to interest rate movements as it can lead to explosion in the funding costs. This also adds to the lack of financial flexibility.

However, there is always the pressure to discover new assets as Talos currently has reserves that are likely to last 5-6 years more. Therefore, it is imperative that Talos continue to discover newer reserves as well. This also has the added risk of revenue concentration in a single geographical area. Natural depletion of oil fields adds to the risk as well.

Therefore amid the existing risks of movement towards alternate energy sources, these factors act as significant headwinds for Talos Energy.

However, Talos has been trying effectively to manage its risk by using the existing infrastructure to acquire newer assets. This leads to Talos trying to discover assets near its existing asset base. While this manages financial risk of heavy capex and upfront fixed cost and ensures quicker time to production, this does not mitigate the risk of revenue concentration in a geographical area.

Geopolitical Uncertainty

While I have already elaborated on how the current geopolitical scenario can be a favourable factor for the business prospect of Talos Energy, there are several aspects in which it can be a source of risk as well. While the current war situation in the middle east has led to increased oil prices and moved the world towards energy security that have acted as effective tailwinds for Talos Energy, there can be other aspects that must not be ignored either. One such primary source of risk is supply-side. Since offshore drilling involves specialized equipment any disruption in the supply chain and transportation logistics can lead to disruption in the production and extraction of crude oil from offshore drilling. Some of the equipment include drilling rigs, subsea systems, floating production platforms, etc. These equipment have fragmented manufacturing across countries.

Moreover, there can be macroeconomic impact as well. The uncertain environment can give rise to tighter monetary conditions, demand destruction due to recessionary environments and inflationary pressures. Moreover, as the global economies move towards renewable sources of energy, it can lead to dampening demand for crude oil in the coming times.

Conclusion

So, overall as the analysis shows, Talos Energy has significant upside opportunity with its proactive cost leadership strategy in offshore drilling which in itself offers significant structural advantages. Moreover, the recent geopolitical environment offers opportunity for earning higher margins due to the increased crude oil prices as well. Having said that, it presents significant risk as well due to the volatility in crude oil prices, supply-chain disruption and other macroeconomic concerns as well as tighter monetary conditions and inflationary impact. So, overall there are multitude of push-pull factors in an industry which involves heavy investment and has high time to production and therefore requires patience to reach breakeven and profitability. In this environment, Talos is positioned relatively secure given its size and proactive cost leadership and operational efficiency along with actively undertaking exploration initiatives. The valuation justifies the argument as well. So, my recommendation for Talos Energy would be BUY.

data.title: Microsoft: The New Standard Oil - Why 'Intelligence Refinery' Is Future Of Global Profit
data.description:
Introduction

When we last looked at Microsoft (MSFT) in June 2025, it was clear that market optimism had pushed the stock price higher than the valuation metrics warranted. At that time, Microsoft was trading at $470, and we set our price target at $365 based on our DCF model. Fast-forward to today, and the company is now trading close to that target.

With new financial data and increased clarity on the current AI 'arms race,' we believe it is a good time to revisit our assumptions and see if this is a good point to add shares again given the recent pullback. In addition, many of the traditional valuation metrics (P/E, EV/EBITDA) are signaling a compelling entry point.

Historical Results (Base for DCF)

To get a clear read on Microsoft's performance, we need to look at the segment results from its FY26 Q2 report (fiscal year). Microsoft continued to show strong top-line sales growth of 17.5% and significant improvement in operating income by 22.6% YTD (six months ended December 31). Operating income was supported by strong margin improvement in Productivity and Business Processes (Office Suite, Copilot, etc.) and More Personal Computing (Windows OEM, Xbox, etc.).

More Personal Computing represents the legacy business, Productivity and Business Processes its bread and butter, and Intelligent Cloud (Azure, OpenAI, and Cloud) is the future rising star for the company. Margins are improving at a faster rate than sales, with a full 2ppt (percentage points) improvement compared to the same period last year.

We know Microsoft's core business is a cash flow machine, but what will drive its valuation to new heights is the new frontier in tech, AI. Therefore, the Intelligent Cloud needs further scrutiny. For us to accurately forecast this segment, we need to have a better understanding of how this segment is evolving and what is in the pipeline beyond looking at different AI LLMs.

Infrastructure and Silicone

Microsoft is rapidly building its infrastructure to support new high-scale AI workloads, resulting in massive CapEx outlays. These have accelerated in recent quarters. The latest earnings call also gives clues to the mindset shift happening in the industry. Microsoft is starting to treat intelligence more like a utility and rapidly adapting to the commoditization of intelligence.

Microsoft's optimization is geared towards the number of tokens per watt per dollar, as the company is pushing to increase utilization (reducing CPU idle times) and decrease Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) by silicon systems and software. The competitive edge, as they see it, is to shape the infrastructure that enables the new high-scale workloads. Essentially being the refinery of the intelligence commodity.

Satya Nadella highlighted some of the ongoing work at the company in the latest earnings call. Overall, Microsoft is pushing its high-cost AI infrastructure to the limit to achieve maximum throughput during peak loads.

"We are building the world’s first AI super factory by connecting our Fairwater sites in Atlanta and Wisconsin via a high-speed AI WAN, effectively creating a single, planet-scale computer." — Satya Nadella

"We added nearly one gigawatt of capacity this quarter alone. We are seeing a fundamental shift where intelligence is becoming a primary utility for every industry." — Satya Nadella

Like the oil titans of the 19th century, Microsoft understands that if intelligence is becoming a commodity, the ultimate competitive edge isn't the product itself; it's the efficiency of the refinery and being the lowest-cost provider that wins.

On the silicon level, Microsoft is also diversifying its supply chain and uses a mix of NVIDIA and AMD and its own Maia chips. This is mitigating to some extent the exorbitant costs of buying the NVIDIA chips that are driving the CapEx to new highs.

The Agent Supermarket

As the industry moves at lightning speed, Microsoft is pivoting its business strategy. It's no longer about simply competing with AI models like ChatGPT; it's about powering platforms like Azure to be the 'supermarket' of agentic AI models.

A new platform need is being born, much like the one we observed in the mobile transition decades ago. Microsoft wants to sit in the center of this massive strategic shift. Customers are starting to expect a wide array of AI models and agents built for specific tasks. Microsoft wants to offer the broadest selection of models of any hyperscaler.

Major Shift in AI Enterprise Usage

AI models are also moving beyond just sitting on a company's data lake. New enterprise demand is focused on models that can capture the knowledge possessed inside the company to absorb the thinking that makes a company unique. Companies want the ability to modify model weights and rewire the AI brain to align with the company's specific way of thinking.

A company today that uses a generic AI model has no advantage over their competitors. However, if they create their own agents and model, they can scale their expertise and create their own IP. This is the new frontier and competitive edge for many companies. A simple way to explain the commercial value of this is that a company can scale the best lawyer at a law firm and apply that person's thinking and know-how at a global scale.

To answer this demand, Microsoft created the platform Foundry to give customers the ability to customize and fine-tune models at their own discretion. Microsoft Fabric brings the streams of data into a massive data lake for harvesting and training. To give another analogy for this, Foundry is the refinery that turns raw data into something valuable, and Fabric is the pumps that bring in the raw oil to the refinery.

We are just scratching the surface of this development, but it is easy to see how commercially valuable this new capability is.

Bringing the Solution to the Masses

Microsoft is also addressing the demand for agent building by regular knowledge workers with Copilot Studio and Agent Builder. No coding knowledge is required, and over 80% of the Fortune 500 companies have active agents built using these tools, according to Microsoft. This is reducing a previous significant barrier that stood between the knowledge worker and the technical coder on the back end. Microsoft has a first-mover advantage in this field as we are entering a new era of macro-delegation and micro-steering across domains via agents. AI agents are transforming the way companies work across healthcare, FMCG, research, etc., and we are just getting started.

We are moving away from the hype of chatbots and into the new age of industrial data.

Microsoft is pulling ahead of the competition and sits in the best possible starting position, as they are already deeply integrated into enterprise solutions. Given how far advanced Microsoft is in the development and the massive potential for TAM expansion, we believe the Intelligence Cloud revenue and margins will continue to improve over time, despite recent quarters slight margin compression, driven by higher investments.

Company Outlook and Analyst Coverage

Management provided updated guidance for the upcoming quarter (Q3). Total revenue is projected to grow between 15% and 17%, though operating margin is expected to be down slightly YoY.

Cloud Gross Margin is expected to be roughly 65%, down YoY driven by heavy investment in AI infrastructure. The Productivity and Business Processes are projected to grow between 14% and 15%. Intelligent Cloud revenue is projected to continue to grow between 27% and 29%. More personal computing is expected to be flat YoY.

Street Estimates

Street estimates from analysts covering Microsoft are quite optimistic, forecasting revenue to grow +18.77% this fiscal year and fall back to 15.54% the following year. Given the run rate YTD is 17.5% and management guidance for Q3 is 16% at the midpoint of the range, we believe the street estimate is too optimistic. This could mean that we are not yet finished with the market sell-off.

DCF Model

Label Q2 2026 - Base Scenario (New) Q2 2025 - Base scenario (Old)
Sales Growth - '26 15.0% 13.8%
Sales Growth - '27 13.9% 13.7%
Sales Growth - '34 12.0% 12.6%
EBIT Margin '26 47.1% 46.7%
EBIT Margin '27 48.3% 47.5%
WACC 9.13% 8.76%
TGR 2.0% 2.5%
CapEx '26 119 Billion 89.8 Billion

We have updated our DCF model to reflect Microsoft's Q2 2026 results and forward guidance. Compared to our previous base case (June 2025), the new base scenario raises revenue and operating margin assumptions. Increased CapEx supports stronger growth in Intelligent Cloud. Proven margin expansion in Productivity and Personal computing warrants an upward revision in margin expectations going forward.

However, valuation headwinds are intensified as a higher WACC is driven by a higher equity risk premium and an increased stock beta. The AI landscape is also becoming more crowded, and we therefore revise down our long-term growth assumptions from 2.5% to 2%. CapEx is updated to reflect the new elevated run rate.

The blue bars represent the latest forecast, and the orange bars are our old forecast.

The increasing CapEx investments weigh on the investor sentiment, but management is maintaining that the spend is demand-driven and not speculative. The backlog continues to exceed supply, and these investments are translating directly into revenue and operating income, supported by the recent financial data.

The AI investments also boost performance not only in Intelligent Cloud but also in the other business verticals like M365. In Satya Nadella’s view, underinvesting represents a bigger long-term risk than the other way around. Failing to build the foundation now could mean that they give away the next-generation cloud leadership to Alphabet or AWS.

The timing and efficiency of converting CapEx into Operating Income significantly impact FCF. We initially doubted the CapEx spend would scale margins so rapidly last year, but we have been proven wrong. A stronger outcome in 2025 has lifted the starting point in our DCF model. Although we have raised the CapEx projection significantly in the coming years, resulting in lower unlevered FCF, the accelerated conversion to cash means that the FCF projection in the outer years exceeds previous projections.

Given the updated projections and the additional information regarding Capex translation to FCF, the new target prices are as follows:

Label Q2 2026 - Base Q2 2025 - Base Current Price
Price Target $443.3 $370.6 $381.1

Recommendation

With the acceleration in sales and improved operating income for Microsoft, we are revising our price target upwards to $443.30 from $370.60 per share. The stock price is below this valuation, and we believe this is an excellent market entry point or an opportunity to buy more shares.

Counterarguments to Our Bullish Stance

In this unprecedented arms race, Microsoft has to accelerate its CapEx even from current elevated levels just to maintain leadership. As a result, we are already seeing a dent in operating margins over the past six months. Should these costs persist or even accelerate, a further margin squeeze is bound to happen. The significant costs of building AI super factories like Stargate could outpace the immediate returns from Copilot subscriptions. If the AI demand ebbs out, Microsoft could end up holding massive, underutilized infrastructure that needs to be sold below book value and create a big drag on the results.

Another threat to Microsoft and the massive CapEx spend is not that demand will ebb, but that other models and solutions will catch up with good enough solutions. AI models with similar capacity as ChatGPT running at a fraction of the cost or compute power will create margin pressure on Microsoft's products. We are already seeing some signs of this from companies like Alibaba, which worried investors about the future of AI, and chip manufacturers like NVIDIA. If AI models start to run on less energy-intense infrastructure, that also makes the massive investments from Microsoft obsolete. Newer AI models like Claude have also proven how quickly the game changes in the new tech frontier of AI.

While we don't know exactly how this will play out, we believe Microsoft's strategic shift towards becoming a platform for AI models, empowering companies to create their own agentic solutions, is the right strategic move to 'sell the shovels' in this new gold rush.

Considering Microsoft's head start in AI and its unique position in the enterprise market, we are confident in its ability to maintain and expand its earnings. Our recommendation is to buy Microsoft today, as this sell-off presents a rare opportunity to buy at a reasonable valuation.

data.title: Cable Eyes 1.3696 After Reclaiming Key Moving Averages, Bulls Defend 1.3500
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

GBP/USD (GBP:USD) has enjoyed a stellar run of late, capitalizing on a weakening US dollar and a shift in global risk sentiment. However, as we move into the tail end of the week, Cable is finding the air a bit thin near the 1.3600 handle, setting the stage for a battle between trend followers and mean-reversion traders.

Daily Chart: Structural Breakout Confirmed

The daily time frame provides the most compelling evidence of a medium-term trend shift. After months of being capped by a persistent descending trendline (dark navy), GBP/USD staged a clean breakout in early April.

Crucially, the pair has successfully reclaimed its 100-day MA (blue) at 1.3444 and its 200-day MA (black) at 1.3413. This "double reclaim" of the major moving averages suggests that the macro bias has shifted from "sell the rallies" to "buy the dips." Currently, the pair is eyeing the next major structural hurdle at 1.3696, which represents a significant historical resistance zone.

The Daily RSI is at 62.7, indicating healthy bullish momentum with plenty of runway before hitting the overbought 70.0 threshold.

GBP/USD Daily Chart, April 16, 2026

H4 Chart: Bulls Defend the 1.3500 Handle

Moving down to the H4 chart, the impulsive nature of the current rally is clear. The pair surged through the 1.3500 psychological level, which had previously acted as a formidable ceiling.

What is particularly encouraging for bulls is the "retest and depart" behavior seen around the 1.3500 mark. The H4 RSI (currently at 65.7) recently flagged a "bear" pivot, leading to a minor cooling-off period.

However, as long as the price remains comfortably above the 1.3378 structural support and the ascending moving averages, the intraday path of least resistance remains to the upside.

GBP/USD Four-Hour Chart, April 16, 2026

H1 Chart: Session Scenarios & Intraday Levels

The hourly chart shows Cable currently consolidating in a tight range between 1.3560 and 1.3585. The 100-MA (blue) on this timeframe is providing immediate dynamic support at 1.3509.

The Bullish Scenario

For the rally to extend in the upcoming session, we need to see a sustained hourly close above the 1.3584 resistance level (the purple line). A successful breach here would likely see a quick test of the 1.3600 psychological barrier, with the door then opening for a move toward 1.3650. Bulls should look for high-volume candles on the breakout to confirm the move isn't a "bull trap."

The Bearish Scenario

The bearish case rests on the potential for a "double top" or exhaustion near 1.3585. If the pair fails to clear this hurdle and slips below 1.3540, we could see a deeper retracement toward the 1.3500 pivot. This area aligns with the 100-period SMA on the H1, making it a critical "line in the sand" for intraday bulls. A break below 1.3500 would signal a broader corrective phase toward 1.3422.

Key Levels to Watch:

  • Resistance: 1.3584, 1.3600, 1.3696
  • Support: 1.3500, 1.3444 (Daily 100-MA), 1.3378

GBP/USD One-Hour Chart, April 16, 2026

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Teledyne: Deserving Of A Premium Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Teledyne (TDY) has a 30% exposure to the defense sector through revenue contributions; 22% of that revenue comes from the US and about 8% from the rest of the world. This massive exposure comes as a result of the firm making strategic acquisitions within this sector, which resulted in YoY revenue growing by more than 40% in Q4 of 2025. Teledyne is a conglomerate firm that acquires small-scale companies in diversified markets that range from the medical sector to scientific research, commercial, aerospace, and defense. In aggregate, these acquired firms offer a wide range of products, from instrumental testing tools, oscilloscopes, and X-ray detectors to infrared sensors, UAV cameras, and water monitoring equipment. In the last 3 years, Teledyne has performed poorly relative to its peers and the broader market. Teledyne returned 48%, while Curtiss-Wright (CW) returned 318%, and Ametek (AME) returns were in line with the S&P 500 returns of 72%.

YTD, Teledyne returned 28% and outperformed Ametek, which returned 14.2%, and also outperformed both the SPY and the (ITA) ETF; both these funds returned 1.78% and 9.63%, respectively. Teledyne's performance closely trailed CW with a percentage point difference of only 4.7%. This positive rebound, after years of stagnant performance, resulted in Momentum re-rating to a B+ and the stock price trading within the sector range of a 21.51x trailing EBITDA multiple. Consequently, the quant system and SA analysts have given this name a hold rating, implying no further upside is to be expected. What is not being appreciated about Teledyne is that, as the firm aggressively grows its aerospace and defense segment, more growth potential will be realized as governments from all over the world continue to increase their military spending. We already see signs of this through multiple contract wins the firm has had. While on the cash flow multiple, Teledyne trades at a premium relative to its median sector, it trades at a discount to the aerospace industry median of 28x, implying a 12% upside. Even though Teledyne is not a pure defense play, given that management is focusing more on growing this segment, viewing Teledyne through the defense sector lens displays a bargain opportunity as peers like CW continue to witness positive momentum while trading at premium prices.

Seeking Alpha has categorized Teledyne under the IT sector and specifically within the electronic equipment industry. Names within this bucket have performed flat YTD and returned 18% in 3 years. Looking at the aerospace and defense names, we can see that the performance has been impressive, with a 98.08% return in 3 years and a 9.55% return YTD. This is due to the macro tailwinds associated with increasing geopolitical tensions, pushing more nations to strengthen their security. Now that Teledyne has a 30% exposure in revenue terms, it will be one of the beneficiaries and hence deserving of a buy rating.

The acquisition chart below shows that while historically the firm has largely made acquisitions in the digital imaging segment, it recently shifted those efforts to aerospace and defense electronics, with the largest acquisitions in this segment made last year in 2025.

These are the benefits of being an acquisition company; this business model gives the firm leverage to diversify its income streams, limiting exposure to a specific sector while simultaneously giving it the flexibility to make entrances into sectors with growth potential. In 2022, through the acquisition of FLIR, Teledyne grew digital imaging revenue 143%, from $986 million to $2.4 billion, that segment revenue has since grown 31.67% to $3.16 billion, accounting for 51% of total revenue. Since 2022, instrumentation revenue grew 24.79%, from $1.17 billion to $1.46 billion, and engineering systems revenue grew 7.37% to $435.70 million, showing the lumpy trend this segment normally experiences. In the same period, the aerospace and defense electronics segment grew by 69%, from $628.7 million to $1.06 billion.

Financial Analysis

FY2025 Income Statement

Looking at the full-year 2025 earnings results, revenue grew 8% to $6.115 billion, with growth led by the aerospace and defense segment, growing 36% YoY, followed by instrumentation and digital imaging, growing 5% and 3%, respectively. The growth was slightly offset by engineering systems showing a 1% decline to $435.7 million. Cost of revenue increased 8% to $3.5 billion, resulting in gross profits increasing 7% to $2.61 billion and marking gross margins of 42.75%, closely trailing the sector margin of 49.32%. Operating expenses increased 1% to $1.46 billion, leading to operating income increasing 16% to $1.15 billion and marking EBIT margins of 18.79%, outpacing the sector margin of 6.83%. Finance and tax expenses increased by 8% and 70%, respectively, resulting in pretax income increasing 17% to $1.09 billion and net income increasing 9% to $895.7 million.

FY2025 Cash Flow Statement

With acquisition companies, I normally include acquisition costs as part of CAPEX, and the reason for this is that, for a conglomerate, making acquisitions is part of the operational activity. If the firm stops making acquisitions, it loses good deals with synergistic benefits, which eventually lead to secular topline and margin declines. So when accounting for acquisition costs, CAPEX increased 353% to $938.7 million, resulting in Adj FCF decreasing 78% to $213 million. Offset by a net working capital outflow of $65.3 million, operating cash flow remained flat at $1.19 billion.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

Teledyne's balance sheet remains to be in good condition. Even though current debt has spiked to $450.1 million while cash reserves decreased 46% to $352.4 million, current assets exceed current liabilities by more than $1 billion. Long-term debt decreased by 24% to $2.03 billion, while shareholder's equity increased 10% to $10.5 billion, resulting in a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.25.

Debt load

Unlike the private equity style of leveraging up the balance sheet to fund acquisitions, Teledyne has actually been reducing its debt levels, which as a consequence has also lowered its interest expenses, creating enough room for margin expansion. On a trailing basis, since Jan 2022, interest expenses have decreased 42.80% from $104.2 million to $59.60 million.

Looking at debt analysis, relative to peers, we can see that Teledyne has one of the lowest debt-to-equity ratios of 0.25 and the second-highest interest coverage ratio of 19.47. All of these factors lessen the amount of risk this name is exposed to.

Profitability

As a result of low debt burdens and moderate operating expenses, Teledyne's margin profile closely tracks the trendline and exceeds its sector margins. The Quant system rated it with a B, while CW got a B+ rating and AME got an A rating. TDY's EBIT and FCF margins sit at 18.98% and 11.22% vs. aerospace EBIT margins of 10% and FCF margins of 5.4% and its industry EBIT margins of 7.6% and FCF margins of 6.6%. Teledyne's margins closely trail CW's EBIT and FCF margins of 19.20% and 13.31%. Ametek far exceeds its peers with EBIT margins of 26.15% and FCF margins of 18.23%.

Valuation

Looking at the valuation metrics, Teledyne seems to be fairly priced, with an EBITDA multiple in line with the aerospace industry bucket at 21x. But when looking at the cash flow multiple, the aerospace and defense median sits at 28.47x, while Teledyne trades at 25x, implying a 12% upside. On the other hand, CW trades at much higher multiples. I think part of the rationale here is that, given that CW is a pure defense play, it is likely to benefit from the abovementioned tailwinds, and investors are pricing that in. Teledyne is not yet viewed with that lens, which gives us an opportunity to front-run that trade as we see the firm winning more defense and military contracts.

Risk

My main bullish thesis on Teledyne is that the company is growing its defense segment through acquisitions and contract wins. Investors should be cautious of the fact that this is a long-cycle segment, meaning that significant revenue contribution might take longer to materialize, and given that the revenue contribution still sits at 30%, it's possible the topline continues growing at mid-single digits. Growth has largely been inorganic and has been offset by demand weakness from the dental market and engineering systems, which stacks integration risks that might cause margin contraction if short-cycle demand doesn't quickly recover. One last risk to the upside is that, with the stock price trading at fair value, any negative news, whether coming from macro events or bad earnings results from the first quarter, could result in a drawdown.

Q1 earnings expectations

Going into the first quarter of 2026, I will be paying more attention to specific sectors within defense that the firm is exposing itself more to, whether through acquisitions or contract wins. One subsector of defense I would urge investors to pay attention to is the unmanned market. Management stated that in 2025 this subsector contributed $500 million to total revenue and expects that in 2026 the contribution will grow to roughly 10% of overall revenue. Given the firm FY2026 revenue guidance of $6.37 billion, this implies that unmanned is expected to grow 27.40% to $637 million. I'm not going to lie, this growth expectation sounds aggressive; this displays management confidence in thinking that Teledyne could win contracts in the LASSO project, as this project is one of many growth catalysts that will come from this market. Monitoring management's guidance in this subsector in the next call will be a tell if the confidence levels remain elevated about the LASSO project. That will influence a huge part of my thesis going forward.

Conclusion

I rate Teledyne a buy due to its increasing exposure to the defense space. Firms like CW, which have similar profitability margins as Teledyne but have higher debt burdens relatively, trade at a premium valuation. The defense segment has performed well in recent years, and I expect more performance even if the Middle East conflict alleviates. The Iran War has brought a harsh reality to many nations: defense matters, and governments should spend as much as their balance sheet can bear to strengthen their military base and protect infrastructure. Even if we could see peace negotiations between the US and Iran, the genie is out of the bottle, and more nations will seek to fund more projects to improve their defense technology. Making firms like Teledyne potential beneficiaries.

data.title: Sea: Why 22x P/E Is A Steal For This Southeast Asian Behemoth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The first time that I covered Sea Limited (SE) was in a comparative article between Grab (GRAB) and Sea. My conclusion in this article was that both were good companies and had a similar profile: by being diversified and being Southeast Asian behemoths that aimed at something close to an “everything app,” but in the end, I chose to assign a “buy” to Grab and a “hold” to Sea.

And in my recent review of Grab, I maintained the buy rating, but I also initiated a position in the company in my personal portfolio. It is a company that I already liked, an exposure that I already wanted to have geographically, but with a much better valuation than ~1 year ago.

I believe that it is more or less the same for Sea.

Since this comparative article, the SE stock fell 40%, and meanwhile, all the revenues of the company increased a lot, and the EBITDA increased even more due to the operating leverage; that is, here we have not only a much lower valuation but also better numbers, which makes Sea a much more asymmetric case now.

Sea: What Happened to Justify the 40% Decline?

The first thing that I thought when I saw a constant drop in the SE stock price was, “Did the company get worse like this? I do not remember the last results having been so bad.”

The answer is no. Sea's business model and financials haven't deteriorated as much as the drop in the stock price might suggest. Actually, the market became more concerned with some points, and the uncertainty increased a bit.

The last quarter reflected these "new concerns" and the new phase of Shopee's industry (more competitive, more aggressive). As I mentioned in the beginning of the article, the growth is still great. Top line grew in all segments and grew a lot; for instance, we are talking about a Shopee growing GMV at ~27%, with Monee and Garena also growing top line close to or even above that.

But the problem is in another part of the income statement. The top line of Sea in Q4 grew 38%. But the costs grew 40%; that is, the company lost gross margin. Even if it has recovered this in the operating income, for the market it was already something more negative.

Most of this increase in cost was due to Shopee, and the management mentioned that the increase in costs was due to logistics costs and more volume. And this is one way to say it. In the earnings call, the management also mentioned the importance of logistics to deepen the moat, with logistics of fast delivery services, partnerships to deliver fresh groceries in 1 hour, etc.

But another way to say it, and it is what the market sees, is that this industry is more competitive, and Shopee is more aggressive. There is an increase in costs that can be seen as purposeful, as aggressiveness, e.g., Shopee sees opportunities to grow or sees that its market share is being “eaten” by movements like TikTok Shop and consequently needs to increase the costs to have a delivery as fast as or faster than its peers and have platforms engaged as much as its peers. This is also evident in the parallel between TikTok Shop (selling through short videos and lives) and the initiatives of Shopee Live. That is, it is not only that the company is increasing its moats; it is also a reflection of how the market is more competitive, and TikTok is just one of them. We can also cite Alibaba (BABA) with AliExpress and Temu of PDD Holdings (PDD), not to mention other players like MercadoLibre (MELI) and Amazon (AMZN) that are also threats in some regions that Shopee operates in (like Brazil).

In this environment, to maintain this GMV growth, Shopee many times needs to increase the coupons, subsidize the shipping, and give an incentive to the seller and to the consumer to either try to capture market share or not lose market share to TikTok/Temu.

Besides that, there was also a considerable increase in credit losses due to “uncollected loans” of the Monee division, which raises one more uncertainty, since even though Monee is doing well, the margin is not at the same pace; that is, this also may have moved the estimates of the market.

In other words, it is not possible to say that the scenario is exactly the same; the environment really is a bit more uncertain, the risks that already existed before now seem a bit more tangible, and the market is pricing this.

Sea's Risks

Summarizing Sea's risks, we’re basically talking about competition, as the section above suggests.

These are markets with potential and growth prospects, but they are also attracting significant investment from various different players.

So Sea needs to execute well, knowing how to balance investing to gain market share, investing to maintain its competitive moats, and knowing when not to enter a market with low margins or avoid getting into a price war that ends up squeezing cash flow.

The other party’s execution is also worth mentioning. Due to some execution error, the company could end up reducing Free Fire’s engagement, and such errors can occur, such as adding features that users don’t like, etc. Consequently, this could trigger a decrease in the value generated by Garena, which is one of the main drivers of EBITDA today.

Sea: Still a Quite Solid Company

Well, as I mentioned, Shopee is still investing to increase its moats, but I think that we need to take a step back and say that Shopee already has wide moats (scale, technology, switching costs—check here). The SPX, which is its own logistics, already has an entire large and functional infrastructure in the main markets of Southeast Asia. What I want to say here is that something that already exists is already working, which infers that Shopee already has an entire expertise in these markets and data, knows the best routes, and has partnerships, while other peers that are entering this market will still have to discover the best “way” to go through the problems that Shopee has already overcome.

And the counterpart of the cost increasing is that Shopee really is building an even more solid infrastructure. I am not saying that Temu will not be able to capture a part of the market share. I believe that they have the scale to be aggressive and try to capture part of the market, but my point is that Shopee really is building something to be a better company in the next years, serve its customers and sellers better (supporting top line growth), and be more efficient (margin expansion).

The part that they mentioned about SPX, which is the own logistics of Shopee, in the earnings call makes what I am saying become a bit clearer:

“SPX Express now processes on average over 30 million parcels every day, making it one of the largest e-commerce logistics solution providers in our market. In 2025, we improved speed and cost efficiency across our markets while customizing delivery options for different user needs. In dense urban areas, we scaled instant and same-day delivery for buyers who value speed and convenience. We expanded instant delivery into additional use cases, including partnering with local supermarkets and suppliers to deliver fresh groceries in Thailand in as little as 1 hour.”

And we have to remember that even though Shopee is the largest part of the revenue (by far), Sea is not only Shopee. Garena is still very important in the EBITDA, and it is a business with potential.

Free Fire, in my view, is not something that it is possible to envision a healthy and growing EBITDA for decades and decades, but it surprised me very positively to see that the Free Fire Bookings grew 30% per year in 2025 (again), not to mention that there are other initiatives like EA Sports FC Mobile that already have a strong performance. So it is not only a cash cow but also has optionalities for growth.

Besides that, even though I have mentioned that the market now has a bit of concern with Monee, fundamentally the adj. EBITDA continues to be quite stable; it is not improving, but it is still a relevant source of earnings.

These 2 divisions provide a great diversification for Sea. Mainly Garena, Sea's entertainment division, which is responsible for managing Free Fire but also developing/publishing/distributing other games, including third-party games. Free Fire, as I said, surprised me positively and definitely stands out; it's a game that focused on distribution and accessibility, becoming a phenomenon in countries such as Brazil, India, and Southeast Asia, as it doesn't require a high-end smartphone or a high-speed internet connection.

As for Monee, Sea’s financial division (payments, bank, loans), even though there is diversification, there is also a great deal of complementarity with Shopee’s business. While there are initiatives like SeaBank and payment services outside Shopee, there is a certain degree of interdependence and synergy for Monee to convert Shopee users, leverage Shopee’s data, and ultimately drive growth for both divisions as part of a single ecosystem.

Putting together Garena and Monee, we have almost 80% of the EBITDA of Sea. Whether we like it or not, even if Shopee is the largest part of the revenue, the most talked about, and what is accelerating a lot, Garena and Monee end up bringing this stability to the shareholder and changing a bit the profile of the SE stock. What I want to say here is not that Shopee is bad, but that Sea Limited is not a pure play of e-commerce/retail; it is more than that.

Bottom Line and Valuation

The icing on the cake here is the valuation. The forward P/E of the SE stock is at 22x, which may seem reasonable, but here we need to take into consideration two things. The first point is that the average of the last 5y is 37x; that is, there is a tremendous discount in the stock. The second point is that Sea is a company with “Grade A” growth. It is growing top line at 30%+ and has prospects of a fwd EBITDA growth of almost 40%.

Another point that is worth mentioning is that the fwd P/E of ~22x is non-GAAP, but different from Western software companies, the stock-based compensation (SBC) is a much smaller problem. The adj. EBITDA of Q4 was $787 million. Removing the SBC, we still have an EBITDA of $646 million; that is, there is a massive shareholder value being generated even considering this dilution.

Another point is that even though other players like PDD have a much better valuation than Sea for being Chinese, we see that the premium is much higher in other companies like GRAB, which trades with a P/E closer to 40x, and even for BABA, which, although Chinese, has a P/E slightly above that of SE stock.

Because of that, I see Sea Limited currently as an interesting move. It has its uncertainties but has a lot of potential with a much more reasonable valuation. That’s why I’m upgrading the rating to buy from hold.

data.title: Electro Optic: A Good Drone Crisis Stock To Buy
data.description:
Shares of Electro Optic Systems (EOPSF) or EOS have more than doubled since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 3% return. It is yet another example of how shifts in defense demand are creating outperforming opportunities for investors layered on top of long-term stable growth in the industry. However, particularly for EOS, we note that while the chances are big the company has a steep execution path ahead to drive demand into backlog, backlog into revenues and revenues into profits. In this report, I discuss whether the investment case for EOS remains attractive.

Counter-Drone Solutions Is What Matter

With the war in Iran, the focus on counter-drone solutions is once again intensifying. In a prior report, I discussed several providers of counter-drone solutions and EOS comes out as one of the top picks with non-kinetic (laser) hard-kill solutions. The company has a portfolio of hard-kill solutions including the Slinger. More promising, however, is the Apollo high-energy-weapon system which uses lasers to take out drones and can provide a more cost-efficient solution against drones.

In the space domain, the company provides optical tracking systems and laser systems that can be used to track space objects. The company’s products, including automated 24/7 tracking and space domain awareness solutions, are quite important for a growing market of missile defense.

So, I believe that EOS is positioned rather well with counter-drone and missile defense capabilities. The company currently has an order book valued at A$459, excluding an $80 million conditional order.

From Future HEL Growth To Today’s Reality

While I am bullish on the prospects of EOS, we note that a higher backlog did not result in higher sales. Revenues declined 27.2% to A$128.5 million while gross margin improved from 48% to 63%. The former is a negative, the latter is obviously a positive. EBIT losses widened from A$13.8 million to A$60.3 million driven by negative forex impacts, lower EBITDA, and non-recurring items. The company’s net profit only increased because of the sale of the EM Solutions business. In that regard, I find it somewhat disappointing to see that EOS marked 2025 as the final year of the turnaround plan, because from an operational perspective they have more than one step to make to become profitable. We also note that the company’s free cash flow burn was A$38.2 million leaving it with A$106.7 million in cash. That is not an uncomfortable position, but to me it is an indication that the company is now at a point where its free cash flow has to start turning positive, ideally by 2027.

So, while the company deems the turnaround complete I believe the turnaround is not meaningful in a financial sense. For the financial growth of the company, what is going to matter is setting up high volume serial production to drive operating leverage.

I Remain Bullish On EOS Stock

For EOS, we see that margins dipped this year. What the company actually announced as the end of a turnaround is more the end of getting its balance sheet in the right spot by selling the EM Solutions business resulting in the EBITDA margin drop as the defense and drone business do not have strong margins yet. In the years after, the pivot to counter-drone solutions should start paying off with free cash flow turning positive by 2027. The net debt leverage is still somewhat messy driven by potential earnout considerations modeled related to the acquisition of MARSS.

Based on an EV/Sales assessment, which I believe is better suited for EOS in the early-stage growth phase, we note that at a peer group multiple, the stock is more or less fairly valued against expected revenues in FY28. However, I do believe that emerging companies with promising products can be valued at twice the peer group multiple and that would show roughly a fair valuation for 2026. My price target is $10.97, indicating 44% upside as I do believe the market should be forward-looking.

Conclusion: EOS Is Positioned Strongly

I believe that EOS is positioned strongly with a portfolio of hard-kill kinetic counter-drone solutions and major upside in hard-kill non-kinetic counter-drone solutions. I believe that the Apollo laser could be a major product for EOS with demand in Europe and the Middle East. Furthermore, the company also can leverage its laser technology for space and missile defense purposes. As a result, I maintain my buy rating for EOS.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nikkei 225's Bullish Reversal Extends Towards New All-Time Highs
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The current US-Iran ceasefire optimism, which is now translating into a higher chance of a peace deal, has ignited the bulls in the Japanese stock market despite the ongoing blockage of the Strait of Hormuz that hinders global oil supply, which, in turn, may rouse stagflation risk.

The failure of the negotiation talks between the US and Iran over the last weekend did not lead to a further escalation of attacks by both sides, but rather some form of compromise to find a “middle ground” as the US and Iran are considering extending the earlier ceasefire deadline agreement, due on next Tuesday, April 21, by another two weeks, and to allow them to make time to set up another round of negotiation talks before April 21.

Nikkei 225 trimmed losses above the key 200-day moving average

Since the start of the US-Iran war, the Nikkei 225 has declined by 13% from the February 27, 2026 high towards a low of 50,395 printed on March 30, 2026, while holding above its key 200-day moving average at around 48,250.

In the past five trading sessions, the losses have been trimmed, and the Nikkei 225 has now recorded a marginal loss of 1.2% measured from February 27, 2026 to April 15, 2026 (see Fig. 1).

Continuation of JGB yield curve bullish steepening has discounted stagflation fear

Since last Monday, April 6, 2026, the shorter-term (2-year) Japanese Government Bond (JGB) yield has declined at a faster pace (6 basis points) versus a drop of 4 bps seen in the 10-year JGB yield.

Therefore, a bull steepening has occurred on the yield spread between the 10-year and 2-year JGBs that led it to trade higher above its key 200-day moving average, acting as a support at 0.84%, to a 15-year high at 1.05% at this time of writing.

A further continuation of a bullish steepening seen in the JGB yield curve is likely to support a further bullish impulse up-move sequence in the Nikkei 225, as both move in direct lockstep since June 2022 (see Fig. 2).

Let’s now focus on the technical factors to determine Nikkei 225’s potential short-term trajectory (1-3 days).

Nikkei 225 - Oscillating within a minor ascending channel

The ongoing 18% rally seen from the March 31, 2026 low on the Japan 225 CFD index (a proxy of the Nikkei 225 futures) is now fast approaching an intermediate resistance of 59,890/60,075 (also the current all-time high printed on February 26, 2026).

Watch the 57,830/57,274 short-term pivotal support to maintain the bullish momentum for the next intermediate resistances to come in at 60,832 and 62,044 (Fibonacci extension clusters) in the first step (see Fig. 3).

On the other hand, a break and an hourly close below 57,274 invalidates the bullish bias for a minor corrective decline within an uptrend phase to expose the next intermediate support at 55,695 (also the 50-day moving average), and below it, may see 55,130/54,600 next (also the 20-day moving average).

Key elements to support the near-term bullish bias on Nikkei 225

  • Price actions are trading above the 20-day and 50-day moving averages.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator hit an overbought reading without a bearish divergence condition.

Original Post
